F E D E R A T E D
L E A R N I N G
Advanced Data Challenge
DANG CONG KHAI
FEDERATED LEARNING FOR LARGE-SCALE AND PRIVACY-PRESERVING AI
HOANG MAI DUC KIEN

MOTIVATION FOR FEDERATED LEARNING
VNUK - INSTITUTE FOR RESEARCH AND EXECUTIVE EDUCATION
Data Transfer Bottleneck: Centralizing massive amounts of edge data
(e.g., continuous video feeds, billions of interactions) is computationally
expensive and strain network bandwidth.
Privacy & Security Imperatives: Users and organizations are increasingly
hesitant to share raw, sensitive data due to the risk of breaches.
Regulatory Firewalls: Strict data privacy laws and compliance standards
restrict the movement of sensitive data across organizational or
geographical borders.

MOTIVATION FOR FEDERATED LEARNING
VNUK - INSTITUTE FOR RESEARCH AND EXECUTIVE EDUCATION
Centralized leads to a reliance on static tiny biased dataset

WHAT IS FEDERATED LEARNING?
VNUK - INSTITUTE FOR RESEARCH AND EXECUTIVE EDUCATION

WHAT IS FEDERATED LEARNING?
VNUK - INSTITUTE FOR RESEARCH AND EXECUTIVE EDUCATION
Traditional deep learning requires copying all data to a central server (like an AWS cluster). Federated
Learning (FL) reverses this: it leaves the data securely locked at its origin and sends the algorithm to the data.
FL operates on the priciple of data minimization. Raw data never leaves the
edge device; only mathematical parameters are exchanged.
Federated learning is a decentralised machine learning technique where AI models are trained across multiple
devices or servers (nodes) holding local data, without exchanging that data. Instead of aggregating raw data
centrally, only local model updates (parameters) are sent to a central server to improve a global model.
FUNCTIONAL ROLES: 
DATA MINIMIZATION

WHAT IS FEDERATED LEARNING?
VNUK - INSTITUTE FOR RESEARCH AND EXECUTIVE EDUCATION

WHY USE FEDERATED LEARNING
User privacy
Train models without sharing data
Data sharing agreements are comple
Richer set of potential system configurations
Data, compute and algorithms can be distributed and decentralized
Cooperation between insitutions and entities (even those that compete)
Lower trust requirements (not just between users)
Utilize data from the edge
Lower requirement for infrastructure

TYPES OF FEDERATED LEARNING 
 BY DEPLOYMENT ARCHITECTURE
Involves thousands to millions of highly constrained
edge devices interacting over unreliable networks.
Mobile keyboards train next-word prediction models
locally based on user typing habits, without
uploading private text messages.
Personalization of Speech Recognition
CROSS-DEVICE

TYPES OF FEDERATED LEARNING 
 BY DEPLOYMENT ARCHITECTURE
Involves a smaller number of highly resourced, reliable
nodes separated by institutional firewalls.
Several hospitals collaborate to train a model that decodes fMRI brain
signals to generate images. The hospitals jointly train the architecture
while keeping the raw patient scans entirely on their own local servers.
Hospitals can use this federated learning to train a model to
detect cancer based on the information from all of the hospitals.
CROSS-SILO

Horizontal  Federated Learning Vertical  Federated Learning
Same features, different users
Examples: 
Smartphone keyboards
prediction
Cancer prediction across
hospitals
Same users, different features
Examples: 
Predict cancer risk based on hospital data +
genetic lab data
Credit scoring: banks + e-commerce
TYPES OF FEDERATED LEARNING 
 BY DATA DISTRIBUTION

TYPES OF FEDERATED LEARNING 
 BY DATA DISTRIBUTION
Horizontal
Federated Learning
Training process 
Local training

TYPES OF FEDERATED LEARNING 
 BY DATA DISTRIBUTION
Vertical  
Federated Learning
Training process

TRAINING ON LOW COMPUTATIONAL-RESOURCE DEVICE
VNUK - INSTITUTE FOR RESEARCH AND EXECUTIVE EDUCATION
LORA
LoRA(Low-Rank Adaption) from https://viblo.asia/p/llm-101-thao-luan-ve-finetune-model-llm-su-dung-lora-low-rank-adaptation-Yym40YQWJ91

DISADVANTAGES
VNUK - INSTITUTE FOR RESEARCH AND EXECUTIVE EDUCATION
Device heterogeneity: each device has different computing power which makes it
hard to train model on each device
Network: Dropped clients, slow wide-area networks
Data is Non-IID:(Not Independently and Identically Distributed): Some clients
might heavily skew the model toward their specific, highly localized data
distributions.
Poisoning Attacks (Robustness): Malicious clients submitting corrupted weight
updates to intentionally degrade the model or introduce backdoors.

MITIGATION APPROACHES
VNUK - INSTITUTE FOR RESEARCH AND EXECUTIVE EDUCATION
Synthetic data generation: rebalance class distributions via oversampling or under-
sampling, SMOTE (non-IID data distributions)
Weight adjustment during model aggregation: instead of weighting only on dataset
size, we also give under-represented clients more influence
Aggregation algorithm: Median aggregation, trimmed mean to mitigate poisoned
attack

VN-UK
@ Institute for Research
& Executive Education

Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
(KAN)

A New Paradigm for Neural Function Learning

The Shift: Moving from learnable linear weights to learnable nonlinear activation functions

The Goal: Achieving superior accuracy and interpretability in scientific machine learning (AI + Science)

Presenters: Department of Artificial Intelligence

Date: April 2026

Speaker Note: Welcome everyone. Today we discuss a fundamental rethink of the MLP, inspired by a 1950s mathematical theorem that might just redefine

Presentation Outline

@ @ EF

1. Problem & 2. Key Insights & 3. Theory & Math
Motivation Core Idea The Kolmogorov-Arnold
The "Black Box" of MLPs Learnable edges and moving Representation Theorem &
and the limitations of existing activations from nodes to architecture formulation.
architectures. connections.

a

4. Performance &
Applications

The Problem —- The "Black Box" of MLPs

»= Dominance: MLPs are the foundational blocks

of everything from Classifiers to Transformers.

= The Interpretability Gap: In architectures like
Transformers, MLPs consume most parameters
but remain uninterpretable without heavy post-hoc

analysis.

= Scientific Accuracy: Standard MLPs often
struggle with high-precision function fitting

required in physics and mathematics.

Speaker Note: We rely on MLPs everywhere, yet they are inefficient at approximating univariate functions and their internal "logic" is notoriously hard to

decode.

Motivation — Why KAN?

» Why now?: Scientists need models that "speak" the language of functions, not just weight matrices.

= The Accuracy Ceiling: MLPs suffer from slow neural scaling laws (N~* or worse), making high-precision

science computationally expensive.

= The Goal: A model that is intrinsically interpretable and breaks the curse of dimensionality for compositional

functions.

Speaker Note: We need a model that doesn't just predict, but helps us discover physical laws.

Limitation of Existing Methods (MLPs)

= Fixed Activations: MLPs use fixed non-linearities (ReLU, GeLU) on nodes; all learning happens in linear

weights.

= Univariate Inefficiency: MLPs are inefficient at approximating simple univariate functions (e.g., exp or sin)

compared to splines.

= Global Plasticity: MLPs have global weights; updating for a new task often destroys previous knowledge
(Catastrophic Forgetting).

Speaker Note: MLPs treat linear transformations and non-linearities as separate entities. This separation limits their local adaptability.

Key Insight — External vs. Internal DOF

External Degrees of Freedom Internal Degrees of Freedom

The computational graph and node connectivity. The shape of the activation functions

This is what standard MLPs possess via their themselves. This is what mathematical Splines
weight matrices. possess.

=» KAN’s Strength: KANs leverage both—Compositional structures (External) and Optimized Univariate

functions (Internal).

Speaker Note: Think of MLPs as having fixed tools but flexible connections. KANs give us flexible tools and flexible connections.

Core Idea — Learnable Edges

=» The Paradigm Shift: Replace every linear

weight w with a learnable univariate function @.
= Mechanism: Nodes no longer "activate"; they

simply sum incoming signals.

s Intuition: Instead of a matrix of numbers, the

network is a matrix of trainable curves. vA

Speaker Note: This is the "Eureka" moment of the paper. We move the complexity from the node to the edge.

oo

Core Idea — Summarized

MLP(x) KAN(x)
W o o(x) (x)
(Linear Weight — Fixed Activation) (Learnable Nonlinear Activation Function)

=» So What?: This allows the model to learn the "shape" of the data distribution directly on the edges.

Speaker Note: By making activations learnable, the network can adapt its very "logic" to the specific function it is trying to fit.

Theory -— Kolmogorov-Arnold Representation Theorem

= The Theorem: Any multivariate continuous function can be represented as a finite composition of univariate
functions and addition.

» Mathematical Breakthrough: It proves that the "only true multivariate function is addition".

= Original Form (Depth-2):

2n + 1
I(x) = Y ®, (
q=1

Speaker Note: This theorem was once considered "practically useless" due to non-smooth functions, but KANs modernize it with deep architectures.

Theory — Breaking the Curse of Dimensionality

= Compositional Sparsity: If a function has a smooth KA representation, KANs can fit it with an error rate

independent of the dimension.

= Scaling Laws: KANSs achieve a scaling exponent of « = k+1 (where k is spline order), outperforming the
standard 1/d scaling of MLPs.

= Technical bound:

| £- fxan || < CG* **”
Where G is grid size.

Speaker Note: This is the mathematical "teeth" of KAN. It scales much faster than MLPs as you increase parameters.

10

Theory — Why This Works

© Hidden Assumption

Most functions in science have smooth, sparse compositional structures.
8 Physics-Inspired

KANSs care about "typical" cases in the physical world rather than mathematical worst-case

scenarios.

Speaker Note: We are betting that the universe is "smooth" enough for KA representations to be learnable.

11

Intuition — The Housing Price Analogy

ft “Lr

1. Multivariate Input 2. KAN Approach
Bedrooms, Square Footage, One learnable curve for
Age of the house. "Bedroom Impact," one for

“Area Impact," etc.

Speaker Note: Instead of a messy multi-variable equation, KAN breaks the problem into single-variable "impact curves" that are easy to see and adjust.

3. Aggregation

Sum these impacts, then pass
through a final "Price

Adjustment" curve.

12

Intuition — Analogy of "Flexible Tools"

MLP Factory

A factory where the machines (nodes) are fixed,
but you can change how much material (weight)

flows between them.

Speaker Note: KAN gives the network the ability to "evolve" its processing units.

I

KAN Factory

A factory where the machines themselves can be
reshaped (splines) to perfectly handle the

specific material flowing through them.

13

Key Insight —- Why KAN Its Interpretable

= Visual Transparency: We can literally plot the
curves on the edges to see what the network has
learned (e.g., it learned a sine wave or a log

function).

= Symbolic Snapping: We can "snap" these

numerical curves into symbolic formulas like

exp(X).

Speaker Note: We are opening the "brain" of the AI and looking at its thoughts as functional graphs.

14

Mathematical Formulation —- The KAN Layer

= Definition: A KAN layer is a matrix of 1D functions ® = {qq p}.
» Input/Output: For n,, inputs and n,,, outputs, we have n,, < No, Univariate functions.

= Node Activation:

3

Ny

Speaker Note: Notice there is no weight matrix W and no bias b. The "weight" is entirely contained within the function @.

15

Mathematical Formulation — Deep KAN

= Composition: A general KAN is L layers deep:

KAN (x) = (®,.; ° ®p_2 © +" oD )x

= Differentiability: Every component is differentiable, enabling standard backpropagation.

= Comparison: While MLPs stack W and o separately, KANs stack functional matrices ®,.

Speaker Note: This allows us to build "Deep Kolmogorov-Arnold" models, something the original theorem didn't envision.

16

Mathematical Formulation — The Activation (x)

= Residual Structure: The core function combines a basis with a spline.

oO(x) =w,b( x) +w, spline (x )

=» Basis Function: b(x) = SiLU(x) (used for global structure)

» Spline Function: Linear combination of B-splines: > c; B;(x)

Speaker Note: We keep a SiLU basis to ensure the network can still behave like a standard MLP if needed, while the spline provides the "fine-tuning."

17

Architecture — Flow Model

= Grid Points: Splines are defined on a grid of G intervals.

=» Local Control: B-splines only change locally, meaning adjusting one part of the curve doesn't affect distant
parts.

» Shape Notation: [no, nj, ..., ny] (represents the number of nodes per layer).

Speaker Note: The architecture is fully connected, but the "weights" are dynamic local curves.

18

Architecture — B-Splines as Activations

» Parameterization: B,(x) are basis functions; c;

are the learnable coefficients.

= Continuity: Splines are smooth and

differentiable, crucial for gradient descent.

= Grid Extension: We can increase the number of

grid points G during training to increase resolution

Ocontrol point

without starting over.

Speaker Note: Splines are the "secret sauce" that allows KANs to be both accurate and stable.

@® knot

19

Architecture — Sparsification Mechanism

L1 Regularization

Applied to the average
magnitude of the activation

functions.

Speaker Note: This is how KAN identifies which variables actually matter in a 100-dimensional dataset.

HK

Entropy
Regularization

Used to prune unnecessary

functional paths.

eR

Visual Result

Unimportant edges fade
away, leaving a "skeleton" of
the true functional

relationship.

20

Architecture — Symbolic Snapping Interface

=» The "Surgery": Users can manually set a learned spline to a symbolic function (e.g.,

fix_symbolic(1,i,j, Sin’ )).

= Human-in-the-loop: The model suggests symbolic candidates, and the human decides which ones make

scientific sense.

ems

Speaker Note: This transforms the researcher from a "data labeler" into a "collaborator" with the AI.

21

Training — Loss and Optimization
= Total Loss:

Liotal = Lprea + A CH, » |D|, TH, ~S(@) )

=» Optimizer: LBFGS is often used for high-precision scientific tasks.

Speaker Note: We balance prediction accuracy with the "simplicity" (sparsity) of the resulting functional graph.

22

Training — Grid Extension Strategy

=» Step-wise refinement: Start with a coarse grid

Speaker Note: This "fine-graining" is unique to KANs and is much more efficient than scaling up MLPs.

23

Training — Learnable Components

= What is trained?:

= B-spline coefficients (c;)
=» Spline scales (w,)
= Basis scales (Wy)

= Batching Issue: Currently, different edges having different functions makes GPU batching difficult.

Speaker Note: While the math is elegant, the implementation requires careful engineering to match MLP speeds.

24

Key Property — Expressiveness

= Pareto Frontier: KANs consistently achieve

lower loss with fewer parameters than MLPs

across special functions and physics datasets. aA 8 co, & | FG H

Speaker Note: KANs don't just win on small tasks; they scale better as the problem gets harder.

$120

29

Key Property — Stability and Continual Learning

= Local Plasticity: Because splines are local, new data only affects nearby control points.

= No Forgetting: In sequential training, KANs preserve old knowledge while learning new tasks, unlike MLPs

which "overwrite" globally.

Speaker Note: This solves one of the oldest problems in AI: Catastrophic Forgetting.

26

Key Property -— Generalization

= Small Models: Smaller KANs often generalize better than larger ones because they capture the "minimal"

symbolic structure.

= Symbolic Alignment: KANs naturally align with the symbolic functions governing the natural world.

Speaker Note: KAN finds the "simplest" explanation, which is usually the most generalizable one.

2/

Comparison — KAN vs. MLP (Architecture)

Activations Fixed, on Nodes Learnable, on Edges

Linear Weights Learnable Matrix None (absorbed in functions)
Complexity O(N?L) parameters O(N7LG) parameters
Activation Basis Global (ReLU, SiLU) Local (Splines)

Speaker Note: KAN has more parameters per edge (G), but usually requires a much smaller network width N.

28

Comparison — Performance Trade-offs

@ Q

Accuracy Speed Interpretability
KAN > MLP (especially for MLP >> KAN (10x faster to KAN >> MLP (intrinsic vs.
science and mathematics) train due to GPU post-analysis)

optimization)

Speaker Note: Choose KAN for accuracy and insight; choose MLP for raw training speed and massive scale.

29

Critical Thinking - Trade-off Reality

= The "Slow" Penalty: Different functions on different edges prevent vectorized batch computation.
= The "Grid" Penalty: High grid resolution G can lead to bad loss landscapes and optimization stalls.

= Insight: KAN is currently a Specialist, not yet a Generalist for massive LLMs.

Speaker Note: Don't replace your LLM's MLP layers with KANs just yet—unless you have a month to train it!

30

Applications — Al + Science

Vx

Mathematics

Rediscoving knot theory
invariants and identifying

important variables.

Speaker Note: KAN is essentially a "Symbolic Regression" tool that actually works on complex data.

:°:

Physics

Solving the Anderson
Localization phase transition

and extracting mobility edges.

[<>]

PDE Solving

KAWNSs solve Poisson
equations 100x more
accurately than MLPs with

100x fewer parameters.

31

Applications —- Scientific Discovery

» Case Study: Relativistic velocity addition:

f(u,v) = Tw

=» Result: KAN auto-discovered the "Rapidity" concept (arctanh) without being told.

= So What?: The model can "teach" the scientist new mathematical representations.

Speaker Note: This is the "AI Scientist Collaboration" paradigm in action.

32

Limitations — The Efficiency Wall

» GPU Batching: Current hardware is optimized for W - x, not for heterogeneous functional evaluations.

= Hyperparameter Sensitivity: Regularization A and grid size G significantly impact the "interpretability" of

the result.

Speaker Note: Engineering is the biggest hurdle. We need custom kernels to make KANs truly competitive in scale.

a3

Limitations -— Theoretical Gaps

= Depth-2 vs. Deep: The original theorem only covers depth-2; the math for "Deep KANs'" is still being
developed.

= Complexity: For high-dimensional non-compositional data, splines may still suffer from the curse of

dimensionality.

Speaker Note: We have empirical success, but the full mathematical theory for deep KANs is an open research field.

34

Open Questions & Research Directions

ST ad C)-

Kansformers Hybrid Models GPU Kernels

Can we replace MLPs in Combining KAN layers with Developing specialized
Transformers to make MLP layers to balance speed hardware acceleration for
attention layers more and accuracy. spline-based networks.
interpretable?

Speaker Note: This is a brand new field. There are dozens of PhD theses waiting to be written here.

Conclusion

» 1. Interpretability: KAN turns the "Black Box" into a "Language of Functions".
» 2. AcCCuracy: Superior Pareto frontiers for scientific and mathematical tasks.

» 3. Collaboration: KAN is a "tool for scientists," not just a "model for data".

Final Thought: If you care about why the model works, use KAN.

Speaker Note: Thank you for your time. KAN is not just an alternative; it's a new way to think about how machines learn.

36

References

« Liu, Ziming, et al. "KAN: Kolmogorov-Amold Networks." arXiv preprint (2024).

= Kolmogorov, Andrei N. "On the representation of continuous functions of many variables by superposition of

continuous functions of one variable and addition." (1957).

» Arnold, Vladimir I. "On functions of three variables." (1957).

⚡  Step  3:  The  Modern  Approach  (FastAPI)  
FastAPI  uses  ASGI  (Asynchronous  Server  Gateway  Interface) .  It  automates  the  "Firewall"  
logic
 
using
 
Pydantic
 
and
 
manages
 
the
 
model
 
lifecycle
 
using
 
the

pattern.

loaded
 
once
 
on
 
startup
 
and

in

What is a Modality?
Modality: the way information is expressed or perceived, e.g., language, vision, sound, touch.
From a sensor: raw modality is closest to the physical signal, abstract modality is derived.
The same phenomenon can appear across multiple modalities (e.g., emotion in voice and 
face).

Heterogeneous Modalities
Information present in different modalities will often show diverse qualities, structures 
and representations.

Dimensions of Heterogeneity

1. Structure

Static, temporal, spatial, or

hierarchical.

4. Granularity

Mismatched sampling rates and

resolutions.

2.Repr. Space

Discrete tokens vs. Continuous

embeddings.

5. Noise

Uncertainty and modality-

specific noise.

VN:UK

Institute for Research
& Executive Education

3. Information

Entropy, density, and

information overlap.

6. Relevance

Task-specific importance of

each mode.

Structure
Static: single images, fixed embeddings.
Temporal: speech, videos, sequential sensor data.
Spatial: 2D images, 3D point clouds, spatial grids.
Hierarchical: document → sentence → word, scene → object → part.

Representation Space
Discrete: tokens, symbolic labels, graph nodes.
Continuous: embedding vectors, feature maps, latent states.
Interpretable vs. latent features (explicit scores vs. black-box features).

Information
Entropy: how uncertain or variable a modality is.
Density: information per unit (per frame, per second, per token).
Overlap and range: shared vs. unique information, and what each modality can 
represent.

Granularity
Different sampling rates: audio (kHz), video (fps), text (tokens/s).
Resolution: high-res images vs. coarse sensors; precision levels.
Granularity mismatch complicates temporal/spatial alignment and fusion.

Noise
Each modality has its own noise distribution and SNR.

Relevance
Not all modalities are equally useful for every task → The importance of a 
modality is context-dependent.
Emotion Detection: Prosody and facial expressions dominate.
Night Driving: Lidar/Radar are more relevant than RGB cameras.
→ Dynamic Weighting: Modern models use gating or attention mechanisms to 
focus on the most relevant modality at any given moment.

Cross-Modal Interactions
Redundancy: multiple modalities carry the same signal (speech + gesture).
Dominance: one modality overrides another when they conflict.
Modulation and emergence: interpretation or new meaning depends on 
combining modalities.

Multimodal Pipeline
Preprocess each modality separately (normalization, tokenization, feature 
extraction).
Encode each modality into latent representations.
Fuse or interact representations, then apply task-specific heads for prediction or 
generation.

Input Module
Modality-Specific Encoders
Text: tokenization → embeddings → transformer encoder.
Image: CNN or Vision Transformer extracting spatial features.
Audio/sensors: temporal models over spectrograms or numerical features.

Fusion Module
The "Heart" of the system where individual data streams are merged.

Early Fusion (Feature-level)
Mechanism: Raw features are combined at the 
beginning of the network.
Pros: Captures high-level interactions between 
modalities early on, simple and parameter-efficient
Cons: Can struggle with strong heterogeneity and 
misalignment (e.g. 60fps videos and 44kHz audio), 
dimensionality explosion, problem with dimensionality 
imbalance.
Example: Concatenating text embeddings with image 
pixels to describe a scene.

Late Fusion (Decision-level)
Mechanism: Each modality is processed by an 
independent model. Only the final 
scores/predictions are combined (e.g., via 
averaging or voting or using a meta-learner).
Pros: Robust; if one modality is noisy (e.g., a blurry 
image), the other (e.g., clear audio) can still carry 
the prediction.
Cons: Loss of correlation, cannot capture cross-
modal interactions.

Intermediate Fusion (Feature-Level)
Intermediate fusion encodes modalities separately but merges their internal "hidden" 
representations (feature vectors) before the final decision-making layer. 
Separate encoders; interaction via cross-attention or shared transformer blocks.
Supports fine-grained alignment (tokens ↔ image patches, frames ↔ sentences).
Common in modern vision–language models.
- Gated Multimodal Units 
- Cross-Attention

Gated Multimodal Fusion
Uses a "gate" (similar to an LSTM or GRU) to decide how much influence each modality should 
have on the final feature representation based on the input context.
Pros:
- Dynamic Weighting: The model can decide to "listen" more to the audio if the image is blurry, 
or "look" more at the video if the audio is noisy.
- Semantic Alignment: Fusing at higher layers allows the model to connect abstract concepts 
rather than just raw pixels.
Cons:
- Architectural Complexity: Requires designing sophisticated interfaces between different types 
of neural networks.
- Parameter Heavy: Often leads to significantly larger models that require careful 
hyperparameter tuning and large-scale training.

Gated Multimodal Fusion

VN:UK

Institute for Research
& Executive Education

Cross-Attention
Instead of a model only looking at its own tokens (Self-Attention), one modality "queries" the 
other.
Pros:
- Dynamic Weighting: The model can decide to "listen" more to the audio if the image is blurry, 
or "look" more at the video if the audio is noisy.
- Semantic Alignment: Fusing at higher layers allows the model to connect abstract concepts 
rather than just raw pixels.
Cons:
- Architectural Complexity: Requires designing sophisticated interfaces between different types 
of neural networks.
- Parameter Heavy: Often leads to significantly larger models that require careful 
hyperparameter tuning and large-scale training.

1. INTRODUCTION

0 1
0 2
0 3
0 4
W h a t  i s  L L M ?
A large language model (LLM) is a type of artificial intelligence
program that can recognize and generate text, among other
tasks.
LLM are very large models that are pre-trained on vast amounts
of data.
Built on transformer architecture is a set of neural network that
consist of an encoder and a decoder with self-attention
capabilities.
It can perform completely different tasks such as answering
questions, summarizing documents, translating languages and
completing sentences
Modern models like GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5, or Claude 3.5 feature
trillions of parameters and context windows up to 1M - 2M tokens.

LLM’s And Its Limitations
--> No
affect by
moderator 
Knowledge Cut-off: Confined to historical training data; struggles with real-time updates.
Hallucinations: Generates factually incorrect but highly convincing and coherent outputs.
Lack of Domain Specificity: Outputs generalized answers; blind to private or internal
organizational data.
Zero Traceability: Incapable of providing accurate source citations for verification.
Prohibitive Retraining Costs: Updating knowledge via Fine-Tuning requires massive
computational resources and time.
Context Window Constraints: Shoving large documents into prompts causes high API costs, high
latency, and the "Lost in the middle" phenomenon.

RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
W H A T  I S  R A G ?
It's an advanced technique used in Large Language Models (LLMS)
RAG combines retrieval and generation processes to enhance the
capabilities of LLMs
In RAG, the model retrieves relevant information from a knowledge
base or external sources
This retrieved information is then used in conjunction with the model's internal
knowledge to generate coherent and contextually relevant responses
RAG enables LLMs to produce higher-quality and more context-aware
outputs compared to traditional generation methods
Essentially, RAG empowers LLMs to leverage external knowledge for
improved performance in various natural language processing tasks

HOW DOES RAG WORK?

RAG: The Detective & The Storyteller

Clues & Evidence

Retriever = Generator =
The Detective The Storyteller

Let’s delve into RAG’s framework to understand how it mitigates these challenges 
G e n e r a l i z e d  R A G  A p p r o a c h

VN-UK
Institute for Research
& Executive Education

RAG ARCHITECTURE

RAG can be implemented in multiple ways, depending on your system’s needs. We
outline each type in the sections below.
R A G  a r c h i t e c t u r e s

RAG Components
An AI framework that allows generative models to access external information beyond their training data.
Combines the strengths of both retrieval-based and generative methods.
Uses a retriever to fetch relevant documents from a large knowledge corpus.
Augments the generative model's understanding to improve the quality of its responses.
Core RAG components:
Retriever
Ranker
Generator
External Data
What is the prompt:
The input provided by the user (such as a question, statement, or text).
Serves as the starting point for the model to generate a relevant and coherent continuation.

Core Concept: Stores data as high-dimensional vectors (Embeddings) to capture semantic
meaning.
Search Mechanism: Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) for fast similarity search, unlike
traditional exact-match SQL databases.
Crucial Pre-step - Data Chunking: Raw documents must be strategically split into smaller "chunks"
before embedding to preserve context quality.
Architecture Trend: Hybrid Search (Combining Vector Search & Keyword/BM25) for maximum
retrieval accuracy.
Popular Engines: Pinecone, Qdrant, Milvus, pgvector.
RAG Components
Vector DataBases

Core Function: The retriever efficiently identifies and extracts relevant information from vast amounts
of data.
How It Works (Relevancy Search): It converts the user's query into a vector representation and
matches it against vector databases using mathematical calculations to find the most relevant context.
Real-World Example: If an employee asks an HR chatbot, "How much annual leave do I have?", the
retriever calculates vector relevancy to fetch both general company policy documents and the
specific employee's past leave records.
RAG Components
RAG Retriever

RAG Components
RAG Generator:
Functions as the core Large Language Model, such as GPT-4, Gemini, or open-weight models like Llama-3.
Generates the final output by synthesizing the user's original query with the retrieved and ranked information.
Ensures the response directly answers the query while accurately incorporating factual, external knowledge.
Update external data:
Continually updates documents and their vector embeddings to keep the retrieval information current.
Real-time Updates: Automatically processes new information instantly to ensure the system reflects the
absolute latest data.
Periodic Batch Processing: Updates data at scheduled intervals (e.g., daily or weekly), providing an efficient
solution for large datasets where instant updates aren't strictly necessary.

RAG BASED CHAT APPLICATION
SEQUENCE FLOW: HOW A USER QUERY TRAVELS THROUGH THE RAG PIPELINE 
AND RETURNS A RESPONSE

1
User Sends Query
The user types a message or question into the Chat App.
This is the trigger that initiates the entire RAG pipeline.
2
Chat App Forwards Query
The Chat App receives the user's query and forwards it to
the RAG system for processing.
3
RAG Retrieves + Generates Response
The RAG model first retrieves semantically relevant
documents from the knowledge base, then passes the
query + retrieved context to the LLM to generate a
grounded, coherent response.
4
LLM Returns Response
Once generation is complete, the LLM returns the final
response back to the Chat App through the RAG layer.
5
Chat App Displays Response
The Chat App presents the generated answer to the user, completing the interaction cycle.
UNDERSTANDING RAG ARCHITECTURE
STEP-BY-STEP WALKTHROUGH

Dimension 🎓 Fine-Tuning 🔍 RAG
🎯 Objective Adapt a pre-trained LLM to a specific task or domain
by updating its weights with labeled examples.
Improve response quality and factual accuracy by
injecting retrieved external knowledge at generation
time.
📦 Training Data Requires curated, task-specific labeled datasets —
high data preparation, time, and compute cost.
Uses a pre-trained LLM as-is, paired with an external
knowledge base that can be updated
independently.
🔄 Adaptability Highly specialized for one domain; adding new
knowledge requires retraining.
Remains general-purpose; knowledge base can be
swapped or updated without touching the model.
🏗 Architecture Modifies model parameters; architecture stays
unchanged.
Adds a retrieval component (vector DB + embedder)
on top of a frozen LLM.
💰 Cost High upfront cost (GPU compute, data labeling). Low
inference overhead.
Low upfront cost. Retrieval adds modest latency at
inference time.
RAG VS FINE-TUNING
TWO DISTINCT STRATEGIES TO ENHANCE

💰 Cost High upfront cost (GPU compute, data labeling). Low
inference overhead.
Low upfront cost. Retrieval adds modest latency at
inference time.
RAG VS FINE-TUNING
TWO DISTINCT STRATEGIES TO ENHANCE LLM PERFORMANCE — 
UNDERSTANDING WHEN TO USE EACH IS KEY

🎯
Enhanced Relevance
Retrieved external
knowledge ensures
contextually accurate and
on-topic responses.
✅
Improved Quality
Grounding generation in real
documents reduces
hallucination and factual
errors.
🔄
Versatility
Adapts to diverse tasks and
domains without task-
specific fine-tuning.
⚡
Efficient Retrieval
Leverages existing
knowledge bases; eliminates
the need for large labeled
datasets.
🔁
Dynamic Updates
Knowledge base can be
updated in real-time without
retraining the model.
🛡
Trust & Transparency
Responses cite authoritative
sources, improving
auditability and user trust.
🎛
Customization
Organizations fully control
which knowledge sources
are included or excluded.
💰
Cost Effective
Avoids expensive full
retraining cycles; compute
cost stays low after initial
setup.
WHY RAG? – KEY ADVANTAGES
EIGHT CORE BENEFITS THAT MAKE RAG A PREFERRED APPROACH FOR PRODUCTION AI APPLICATIONS

💬
Conversational AI / Chatbots
RAG enables chatbots to pull real-time
information from product docs, FAQs, and
knowledge bases — delivering accurate,
contextual replies beyond static training data.
❓
Advanced Question Answering
Retrieves the most relevant passages from large
document corpora (legal texts, research papers,
manuals) to construct precise, evidence-backed
answers.
✍
Content Generation
Augments article writing, summarization, and
content recommendation with retrieved facts,
statistics, and examples from verified external
sources.
🏥
Healthcare & Research
Assists clinicians and researchers in accessing
the latest medical literature, clinical guidelines,
and trial data — keeping them informed with
current evidence.

INDUSTRIES AND SCENARIOS WHERE RAG DELIVERS MEASURABLE, PRODUCTION-READY VALUE

The Lab to Factory Gap: Failure Modes in Production

Traditional ML fails due to system instability, not algorithmic inaccuracy.
Deploying an Al system exposes three core failure modes:

Deterministic

Code

Data & Concept Drift

e The world changes. Covariate
Shift alters input distributions
P(X), while Concept Drift alters
the statistical relationship

between inputs and targets P(Y|X).

Silent System Failures

Silent Training-Serving Skew

e Divergence between batch training
pipelines and real-time serving
APIs. Inconsistent feature
calculation logic destroys input
integrity.

Pa tal’

The Reproducibility Crisis

e Disconnected scripts, hidden
configurations, and unversioned
data make it impossible to

The Architecture Trilemma:
Production Trade-offs

Optimizing a single metric leads to systemic failure.
MLOps is multi-objective optimization.

Accuracy vs. Latency Freshness vs. Stability

Increasing model complexity (FP32) boosts Blind, constant retraining creates short-term
offline accuracy but breaks latency budgets. noise and instability.

e Solution: Quantization (INT8), Mixed e Solution: Accuracy-aware retraining
Precision, and streamlined feature lookup. triggers (CT) and warm-starting weights.

Complexity vs. Maintainability
Tangled code and microservice bloat create crushing technical debt.

e Solution: Clean architecture separating data, feature, and model
pipelines. Scale to Kubernetes only when necessary.

e Infrastructure as Code (Terraform): GCS buckets, BigQuery datasets, Artifact Registry,
and Endpoints are managed via parameterized modules (prod.tfvars).

e Zero-Trust IAM: Least privilege Service Accounts (SA). Isolated SAs for Data Scientists,
Automation (CI/CD), and Inference.

Networking: VPC Peering and Private Service Connect ensure data never traverses the
public internet.

[GCP Blueprint] Feature Management (BigQuery & Bigtable)

Preventing Training-Serving Skew using GCP’s native data stack.

Model Training

BigQuery
(Offline)

Prediction
Endpoint

Bigtable
(Online)

Streaming Sync

The Single Source of Truth: BigQuery acts as the core Offline Store. Feature engineering utilizes
parallelized BigQuery SQL.

Standardized Schema: Tables strictly enforce entity_id, feature_values, and feature_timestamp
to enable point-in-time lookup and definitively eliminate data leakage.

Feature Registry Governance: Groups features, monitors statistics, and triggers alerts on Feature Drift.

Online Serving (Bigtable): Vertex Al syncs features from BigQuery to Bigtable for <10ms serving latency.
Supports Batch Sync and Continuous Queries for streaming updates (vital for GenAl Vector Search).

[GCP Blueprint] Pipeline Orchestration (Vertex Al)

Serverless pipeline execution using Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP).

e Google Cloud Pipeline Components (GCPC):

Pre-built modules for standard GCP actions
minimizing custom code overhead.

Conditional Execution (ds1.Condition): The
production gatekeeper. Ensures deployment nodes

only trigger if

evaluation_metrics.accuracy > threshold.

e Vertex ML Metadata: Automatic lineage tracking.
Visually maps exactly which BigQuery dataset and

Git commit produced the current production

endpoint
dsI.Condition Step Caching: Auto-reuses cached outputs for
Deploy_Endpoint unchanged components, drastically reducing

compute costs and iteration time.

Event-driven architecture to autonomously combat Model Decay.

Detects Drift Validation Gate
KFP
Vertex Model Cloud = ;
Monitoring pun ee Functions "Pineling. x Saleh Toki

e Vertex Al Model Monitoring: Scans prediction streams using TFDV algorithms

(Jensen-Shannon for numerical, L-infinity for categorical). Also tracks Feature Attribution (SHAP)
drift.

e Granular Thresholds: Tight thresholds (0.1) for critical financial features, loose thresholds
(0.5) for noisy/seasonal features.

e Event-Driven CT Flow: 1. Drift breaches threshold. 2. Cloud Monitoring fires alert to Pub/Sub.
3. Cloud Function evaluates severity and triggers KFP Retraining Pipeline.

e Validation Gate: The new model automatically replaces the old only if metric improvements
exceed a strict margin.

[GCP Blueprint] Security, Compliance & Governance

Applying Zero-Trust architecture to the ML Lifecycle.

IAM / Workload
Identity

Cloud KMS
/ DLP

VPC Service Controls (VPC-SC): Isolates Vertex
AI, BigQuery, and GCS from the public internet.
Prevents data exfiltration and model theft.

Cloud DLP (Data Loss Prevention): Automatically
intercepts data streams, scanning and
masking/redacting Pll before it enters the training
pipeline.

Workload Identity Federation: Cl/CD pipelines
assume short-lived SA tokens. No static .json keys
exist in code.

Customer-Managed Encryption Keys (CMEK):
Integrated with Cloud KMS. Revoking the key
instantly cryptographically shreds access to the
model and data.

Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) & Rate Limiting:
Secures endpoints from DDoS and unauthorized
inference attacks.

“Machine Learning Operations is not about making models run faster;
it is about making systems fail safely and recover automatically.”

By marrying academic design principles with cloud-native infrastructure, organizations transform Al from a
